sentences.base_language: It was Nicolas's thirty-fifth birthday and his friends had been secretly planning a surprise party for weeks.
sentences.target_language: C'était le trente-cinquième anniversaire de Nicolas et ses amis planifiaient secrètement une fête surprise depuis des semaines.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Depuis des semaines** — **depuis** + imparfait to express an ongoing action that had been continuing up to the past reference point: **planifiaient depuis des semaines** (had been planning for weeks). Unlike English, which uses the past perfect progressive, French uses the imparfait + **depuis** to describe an action that was still in progress at the moment of narration: **il travaillait depuis trois ans** (he had been working for three years). Compare: **depuis** + present tense for actions still ongoing at the moment of speaking: **il planifie depuis des semaines** (he has been planning for weeks).
**Trente-cinquième** — ordinal numbers in French are formed by adding **-ième** to the cardinal number: **trente-cinq** → **trente-cinquième**. Note the insertion of **-u-** before **-ième** for numbers ending in **-e**: **quatre** → **quatrième**, **onze** → **onzième**. Ordinal numbers agree in gender and number with the noun they modify: **le trente-cinquième anniversaire** (masculine), **la trente-cinquième page** (feminine). **Anniversaire** (m.) = birthday or anniversary — context distinguishes the two meanings.

sentences.base_language: He spent the morning convinced it would be a quiet day — he had told everyone he did not want any fuss.
sentences.target_language: Il a passé la matinée convaincu que ce serait une journée tranquille — il avait dit à tout le monde qu'il ne voulait pas d'histoires.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ce serait** — conditional in reported speech: the original thought was **ce sera** (it will be — future indicative), but after a past reporting verb (**convaincu** implying a past state of mind), the future shifts to the conditional: **ce serait** (it would be). This tense concordance applies consistently after past verbs of saying, thinking, or believing: **il croyait que ce serait facile** (he thought it would be easy). Compare: **il croit que ce sera facile** (present → future maintained).
**Ne voulait pas d'histoires** — partitive after negation reduced to **de**: **ne voulait pas d'histoires** (didn't want any fuss — lit. didn't want any stories/drama). After a negative construction, the partitive article (**du, de la, de l', des**) and the indefinite article **un/une** all reduce to **de** (or **d'** before a vowel): **il ne veut pas de problèmes** (he doesn't want any problems). **Des histoires** = fuss, drama, trouble — idiomatic plural: **faire des histoires** (to make a fuss), **pas d'histoires !** (no fuss! / no trouble!).

sentences.base_language: His closest friend called in the afternoon and casually suggested coming over for a simple dinner together.
sentences.target_language: Sa meilleure amie l'a appelé dans l'après-midi et lui a suggéré en passant de venir dîner simplement ensemble.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a suggéré** — indirect object pronoun **lui** (to him) placed before the auxiliary: **lui a suggéré** (suggested to him). **Suggérer à quelqu'un de + infinitif** = to suggest to someone that they do something: **lui a suggéré de venir** (suggested to him that he come / suggested he come over). The structure is: **suggérer à** + person + **de** + infinitive. The indirect object pronoun replaces **à + person** before the verb: **a suggéré à Nicolas** → **lui a suggéré**.
**En passant** — gérondif used idiomatically to mean 'casually, in passing, offhandedly': **a mentionné en passant** (mentioned in passing). The gérondif (**en** + present participle) here conveys the manner in which she made the suggestion — as if it were a casual afterthought rather than a deliberate plan. **En passant** is a fixed expression meaning incidentally or casually. Compare: **en souriant** (smilingly), **en hésitant** (hesitatingly) — these describe manner more literally.

sentences.base_language: When Nicolas arrived and the door opened, a room full of people shouted "Surprise!" all at once.
sentences.target_language: Quand Nicolas est arrivé et que la porte s'est ouverte, une salle pleine de monde a crié « Surprise ! » tous en même temps.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est arrivé** — **arriver** takes **être** as auxiliary in the passé composé: **est arrivé** (arrived — masculine singular, no additional ending). Movement and change-of-state verbs (the so-called 'DR and MRS VANDERTRAMP' group) take **être**: **arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, naître, mourir, rester, tomber**, and all pronominal verbs. The past participle agrees with the subject: here **Nicolas** is masculine singular, so **arrivé** takes no additional agreement marker.
**Que la porte s'est ouverte** — after an initial **quand** clause, a second coordinated temporal clause uses **que** instead of repeating **quand**: **quand… et que…** This is a standard grammatical rule in French: **quand il est entré et que la lumière s'est allumée** (when he entered and the light came on). **S'est ouverte** — pronominal verb **s'ouvrir** (to open — of a door opening by itself or being opened): **s'est ouverte** (opened — feminine, agreeing with **la porte**). **S'ouvrir** vs **ouvrir**: **ouvrir** is transitive (someone opens something); **s'ouvrir** is intransitive (something opens).

sentences.base_language: His heart leaped and he felt tears spring to his eyes — he had had absolutely no idea.
sentences.target_language: Son cœur a fait un bond et il a senti les larmes lui monter aux yeux — il n'avait absolument pas eu vent de quoi que ce soit.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui monter aux yeux** — **faire/sentir + infinitif** construction: **a senti les larmes lui monter aux yeux** (felt tears spring to his eyes — lit. felt tears rise to his eyes). **Sentir** + noun + infinitive expresses perceiving something happening: **il a senti le sol trembler** (he felt the ground shake), **elle a senti la peur la gagner** (she felt fear taking hold of her). The indirect object pronoun **lui** (to him) indicates to whose eyes the tears were rising — an idiomatic way of expressing physical sensation.
**N'avait pas eu vent de quoi que ce soit** — plus-que-parfait in a negative construction: **n'avait pas eu vent** (had had no inkling / had not gotten wind). **Avoir vent de quelque chose** = to get wind of something, to hear about something: **il n'avait pas eu vent du projet** (he had no idea about the project). **Quoi que ce soit** = anything at all, anything whatsoever — a reinforced negative complement: **ne… pas quoi que ce soit** (not a single thing / absolutely nothing). The plus-que-parfait situates this state of ignorance as having persisted up to the moment of arrival.

sentences.base_language: The living room was decorated with flowers and colourful lights, and his favourite music was already playing.
sentences.target_language: Le salon était décoré de fleurs et de lumières colorées, et sa musique préférée était déjà en train de jouer.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Était décoré de** — statal passive in the imparfait + **de** after a past participle: **décoré de fleurs** (decorated with flowers). When a past participle indicates the material or means of decoration, French uses **de** rather than **avec**: **orné de** (adorned with), **parsemé de** (strewn with), **couvert de** (covered with). Compare: **décoré avec soin** (decorated with care — **avec** when indicating manner or instrument). The imparfait **était décoré** describes an ongoing background state at the moment of Nicolas's arrival, not a one-time action.
**Était en train de jouer** — **être en train de + infinitif** in the imparfait to emphasize an action in progress at a specific past moment: **était en train de jouer** (was playing / was in the process of playing). **Être en train de** + infinitive is the French progressive construction, expressing an action actively underway. In the imparfait, it reinforces the sense of an action caught mid-flow: **il était en train de parler** (he was in the middle of talking). Compare: **jouait** (was playing — also correct, but **être en train de** adds emphasis on the ongoing nature).

sentences.base_language: Friends from every stage of his life were there — old schoolmates, colleagues, cousins he hadn't seen in years.
sentences.target_language: Des amis de toutes les périodes de sa vie étaient là — d'anciens camarades de classe, des collègues, des cousins qu'il n'avait pas vus depuis des années.
sentences.grammarNote:
**D'anciens camarades** — **ancien** placed before the noun means 'former, old': **d'anciens camarades de classe** (old/former classmates). When **ancien** follows the noun, it means 'ancient, antique': **un bâtiment ancien** (an old/ancient building). This pre- vs post-nominal placement distinction is a key feature of French adjectives: compare **un grand homme** (a great man) vs **un homme grand** (a tall man). The article **des** becomes **de** before a plural adjective: **des amis** → **de bons amis**, **des camarades** → **d'anciens camarades**.
**Qu'il n'avait pas vus** — plus-que-parfait with past participle agreement in a relative clause: **qu'il n'avait pas vus** (that he hadn't seen — masculine plural). The relative pronoun **qu'** is the direct object of **voir** and precedes the verb in the plus-que-parfait; the past participle **vus** agrees with this preceding direct object (**cousins**, masculine plural, adding **-s**). This agreement rule applies whenever the direct object precedes the verb: **les amis qu'il avait invités** (the friends he had invited).

sentences.base_language: Nicolas was overwhelmed with gratitude and went around the room embracing everyone and thanking them.
sentences.target_language: Nicolas était submergé de gratitude et a fait le tour de la pièce en étreignant tout le monde et en les remerciant.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En étreignant… en les remerciant** — double gérondif expressing two simultaneous actions accompanying the main verb: **a fait le tour en étreignant et en les remerciant** (went around… embracing… and thanking them). When two gérondifs are coordinated with **et**, the **en** is repeated before each: **en chantant et en dansant** (singing and dancing). The gérondif expresses manner — how he went around the room. **Étreindre** = to embrace, to hug warmly (more intense than **embrasser**): **étreindre quelqu'un** implies a heartfelt, close embrace.
**Les remerciant** — direct object pronoun **les** (them) placed before the present participle in the gérondif: **en les remerciant** (thanking them). Object pronouns precede the verb in all non-imperative forms, including the gérondif: **en le regardant** (looking at him), **en lui parlant** (speaking to him). **Faire le tour de** = to go around, to do a circuit of: **faire le tour de la pièce** (go around the room), **faire le tour du monde** (go around the world) — a common idiomatic construction.

sentences.base_language: The table was covered with his favourite dishes, including the gratin dauphinois his mother always made for special occasions.
sentences.target_language: La table était couverte de ses plats préférés, dont le gratin dauphinois que sa mère faisait toujours pour les grandes occasions.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dont le gratin dauphinois** — relative pronoun **dont** replacing **de** + noun: **parmi lesquels le gratin dauphinois** could also work, but **dont** is more natural when the antecedent includes something more loosely defined as part of a group. Here **dont** functions as 'including, among which': **ses plats préférés, dont le gratin dauphinois** (his favourite dishes, including the gratin dauphinois). **Dont** replaces **de + antecedent** in a relative clause and can express inclusion, possession, or reference to a verbal complement in **de**.
**Faisait toujours** — imparfait expressing habitual action in the past: **faisait toujours** (always made / used to always make). The imparfait is the tense for repeated, habitual, or customary past actions: **il prenait toujours son café noir** (he always drank his coffee black). Contrast with the passé composé, which marks a specific completed event: **elle a fait un gratin hier** (she made a gratin yesterday). **Les grandes occasions** = special occasions — lit. 'the great occasions'; **grande** here means important, significant rather than large in size.

sentences.base_language: Someone poured glasses of champagne and everyone raised a toast to Nicolas's health and happiness.
sentences.target_language: Quelqu'un a rempli des coupes de champagne et tout le monde a levé son verre à la santé et au bonheur de Nicolas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A rempli des coupes de champagne** — **remplir quelque chose de quelque chose** = to fill something with something: **a rempli des coupes de champagne** (filled glasses with champagne). **De** introduces the substance used to fill the container: **remplir un verre d'eau** (fill a glass with water), **remplir une assiette de riz** (fill a plate with rice). Note: **une coupe** (f.) = a flute or coupe glass — the appropriate word for a champagne glass; **un verre** is the general word for glass.
**À la santé et au bonheur de Nicolas** — the standard French toast formula: **lever son verre à la santé de** (raise one's glass to the health of). The contracted form **au** = **à + le**: **au bonheur** (à + le bonheur). **À la santé de** is the classic toast: **à ta santé !** / **à votre santé !** (cheers! — lit. to your health!). The preposition **à** governs both **la santé** and **le bonheur** — when coordinating two nouns with different articles, each article is maintained: **à la santé et au bonheur**.

sentences.base_language: Nicolas gave a short, heartfelt speech and tried to put into words how much the people in that room meant to him.
sentences.target_language: Nicolas a prononcé un petit discours sincère et a essayé de mettre en mots à quel point les personnes présentes dans cette pièce comptaient pour lui.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À quel point… comptaient** — indirect question with imparfait embedded in a past narrative: **a essayé de mettre en mots à quel point… comptaient** (tried to put into words how much… meant). **À quel point** introduces an embedded interrogative clause (indirect question): **à quel point il était heureux** (how happy he was). After a past main verb, the embedded clause uses the imparfait for states: **comptaient pour lui** (meant to him / mattered to him — imparfait for an ongoing state).
**Prononcer un discours** = to give/deliver a speech: **prononcer un discours** is the standard collocation in French (not **faire un discours**, though that is also used in informal contexts — **prononcer** is more formal and precise). **Compter pour quelqu'un** = to matter to someone, to mean something to someone: **tu comptes beaucoup pour moi** (you mean a lot to me). **Mettre en mots** = to put into words — a calque of the English expression, now established in French: **il est difficile de mettre en mots ce qu'on ressent** (it is hard to put into words what one feels).

sentences.base_language: As the evening wore on, someone turned the music louder and the living room became an impromptu dance floor.
sentences.target_language: Au fil de la soirée, quelqu'un a monté le son de la musique et le salon s'est transformé en piste de danse improvisée.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Au fil de la soirée** = as the evening wore on, as the evening progressed — lit. 'along the thread of the evening'. **Au fil de** is an idiomatic expression of time meaning as time passes or along the course of: **au fil des années** (over the years / as the years passed), **au fil du temps** (as time goes on). It conveys a sense of gradual, continuous progression — more evocative than the simpler **au cours de la soirée** (during the evening).
**S'est transformé en** — pronominal verb **se transformer en** = to turn into, to become: **s'est transformé en piste de danse** (became a dance floor / turned into a dance floor). **Se transformer en** + noun (no article after **en**): **se transformer en monstre** (turn into a monster), **se transformer en succès** (turn into a success). Note the absence of an article after **en** when indicating a transformation or role: **servir de** (serve as), **se prendre pour** (take oneself for). **Monter le son** = to turn up the volume: **monter** = to turn up; **baisser le son** = to turn down the volume.

sentences.base_language: His feet and back began to ache from dancing but he could not bring himself to stop.
sentences.target_language: Ses pieds et son dos ont commencé à lui faire mal à force de danser, mais il n'arrivait pas à s'arrêter.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À force de danser** — **à force de + infinitif** = by dint of doing, from doing (too much of something): **à force de danser** (from dancing so much / through all the dancing). This expression indicates that the result (aching) was caused by the prolonged repetition of an action: **à force de travailler, il s'est épuisé** (by working so hard, he exhausted himself). Compare: **à cause de** (because of — for external causes) vs **à force de** (through the repeated or intensive action of).
**N'arrivait pas à s'arrêter** — **arriver à + infinitif** in the negative = to manage to do, to be able to bring oneself to do: **n'arrivait pas à s'arrêter** (couldn't bring himself to stop / couldn't manage to stop). **Arriver à** + infinitive expresses successfully managing to do something; in the negative, the inability or failure to do it: **je n'arrive pas à le croire** (I can't bring myself to believe it). The imparfait **n'arrivait pas** describes a persistent state during this period of dancing. **S'arrêter** = to stop (intransitive, pronominal): **s'arrêter de danser** (to stop dancing).

sentences.base_language: When the birthday cake appeared — a magnificent three-tiered chocolate cake ablaze with candles — everyone began to sing.
sentences.target_language: Quand le gâteau d'anniversaire a fait son apparition — un magnifique gâteau au chocolat à trois étages couvert de bougies — tout le monde s'est mis à chanter.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A fait son apparition** — idiomatic construction meaning 'appeared, made its appearance': **a fait son apparition** (appeared — lit. made its appearance). **Faire son apparition** is more vivid and dramatic than **est apparu** (appeared), implying a notable, anticipated arrival: **le soleil a fait son apparition** (the sun appeared / made its appearance). **Faire son entrée** is a similar construction: **le chef a fait son entrée** (the chef made his entrance).
**S'est mis à chanter** — **se mettre à + infinitif** = to begin to do, to start doing (often implying a sudden or spontaneous start): **s'est mis à chanter** (started singing / began to sing). **Se mettre à** + infinitive in the passé composé marks the onset of an action: **elle s'est mise à pleurer** (she started crying), **ils se sont mis à danser** (they started dancing). Compare: **commencer à** (to start — more neutral) vs **se mettre à** (to set about doing — implying sudden commencement or a new activity). Note: **se mettre à** past participle **mis** agrees with the subject in the pronominal passé composé.

sentences.base_language: Nicolas closed his eyes, made a silent wish, and blew out all the candles in one breath.
sentences.target_language: Nicolas a fermé les yeux, a fait un vœu en silence et a soufflé toutes les bougies d'un seul souffle.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A fermé les yeux** — **fermer les yeux** with definite article, not possessive: **a fermé les yeux** (closed his eyes), not *a fermé ses yeux*. In French, when referring to body parts with a reflexive or self-evident possessive context, the definite article replaces the possessive adjective: **elle lève la main** (she raises her hand), **il hoche la tête** (he nods his head). The possessive is clear from context (whose eyes would he close?). This construction is standard for body part references in French.
**D'un seul souffle** = in one breath — lit. 'with a single breath': **d'un seul souffle** (in one breath). **D'un** introduces a single means or manner: **d'un coup** (in one go), **d'un seul regard** (with a single glance), **d'une seule voix** (with one voice). **Souffler les bougies** = to blow out the candles — the standard expression for blowing out birthday candles in French: **souffler ses bougies** (blow out one's candles) is the birthday ritual. **Faire un vœu** = to make a wish: **faire un vœu** is the standard collocation.

sentences.base_language: The cake was extraordinary — dense, moist, and filled with a cream that tasted like childhood.
sentences.target_language: Le gâteau était extraordinaire — dense, moelleux, et fourré d'une crème qui avait le goût de l'enfance.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Fourré de** — past participle **fourré** (filled, stuffed) + **de**: **fourré d'une crème** (filled with a cream). **Fourrer de** = to fill with, to stuff with — used for cakes, pastries, sandwiches: **un gâteau fourré à la confiture** (a jam-filled cake), **un chocolat fourré** (a filled chocolate). Note that **fourré de** vs **fourré à**: **fourré de** + indefinite article introduces a substance filling the whole; **fourré à** + definite article names the flavour or type of filling: **fourré à la crème** (cream-filled), **fourré au chocolat** (chocolate-filled).
**Avait le goût de l'enfance** — **avoir le goût de** = to taste of, to have the taste of: **avait le goût de l'enfance** (tasted like childhood — lit. had the taste of childhood). **Avoir le goût de** takes a noun with a definite article for a category or abstract quality: **avoir le goût de la liberté** (taste like freedom), **avoir le goût d'autrefois** (taste of the past). Compare: **avoir un goût de** + noun (no article) for a more literal flavour: **avoir un goût de fraise** (taste of strawberry). **Moelleux** = moist, soft, tender — used for cake texture: **un gâteau moelleux** (a moist/tender cake) — also used for texture of bread or a sofa.

sentences.base_language: Long after midnight, the last guests began saying their goodbyes with long embraces at the door.
sentences.target_language: Bien après minuit, les derniers invités ont commencé à prendre congé avec de longues accolades sur le pas de la porte.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Prendre congé** = to take one's leave, to say goodbye — a formal or slightly elevated expression: **prendre congé de quelqu'un** (to take leave of someone). More formal than **dire au revoir** (say goodbye) or **partir** (leave), **prendre congé** implies a courteous, ceremonial parting: **il a pris congé de ses hôtes** (he took leave of his hosts). This register suits the warmth and ceremony of the occasion.
**Sur le pas de la porte** = on the doorstep, at the door — idiomatic: **sur le pas de la porte** (on the threshold / in the doorway). **Le pas de la porte** = the threshold, the doorstep — a fixed expression: **rester sur le pas de la porte** (linger in the doorway), **discuter sur le pas de la porte** (chat on the doorstep). **Bien après minuit** = well after midnight — **bien** used as an intensifier before **après**: **bien avant** (well before), **bien au-delà** (well beyond). **Accolade** (f.) = embrace, hug (especially a warm farewell one) — distinct from the general **câlin** (cuddle) or **étreinte** (embrace).

sentences.base_language: When everyone had finally left, Nicolas stood alone in the quiet room, still glowing with warmth and happiness.
sentences.target_language: Quand tout le monde fut enfin parti, Nicolas se retrouva seul dans la pièce silencieuse, encore rayonnant de chaleur et de bonheur.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Fut enfin parti / se retrouva** — passé antérieur and passé simple in literary narrative: **fut parti** (passé antérieur of **partir**: had finally left) and **se retrouva** (passé simple of **se retrouver**: found himself). The passé antérieur is used in literary/formal written French after time conjunctions (**quand, lorsque, dès que, aussitôt que**) to express an action immediately preceding the main past simple action: **quand il fut parti, elle pleura** (when he had left, she wept). In spoken or informal French, the plus-que-parfait and passé composé replace these tenses.
**Rayonnant de** — present participle **rayonnant** used as an appositive adjective: **rayonnant de chaleur et de bonheur** (glowing with warmth and happiness). **Rayonner de** = to radiate, to glow with: **rayonner de bonheur** (radiate happiness / glow with happiness), **rayonner de fierté** (beam with pride). The present participle used appositively is invariable in French (unlike adjectives) but here **rayonnant** functions adjectivally and so agrees with the subject: **Nicolas… rayonnant** (masculine singular — though the form is identical to the invariable participle here, strict grammar treats it as an adjective in agreement).

sentences.base_language: He sent a long voice message to his closest friend, thanking her for having organized everything so perfectly.
sentences.target_language: Il a envoyé un long message vocal à sa meilleure amie, en la remerciant d'avoir tout organisé avec autant de soin.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En la remerciant d'avoir organisé** — gérondif expressing manner or accompanying action + **remercier quelqu'un d'avoir + past infinitive**: **en la remerciant d'avoir tout organisé** (thanking her for having organized everything). **D'avoir organisé** is the past infinitive (**avoir** + past participle), used after **remercier de** when the action being thanked for is already completed: **merci d'être venu** (thank you for having come / thank you for coming). The past infinitive is used when the action of the infinitive precedes the action of the main verb. **La** (direct object, feminine) refers to his friend and precedes the verb in the gérondif.
**Avec autant de soin** = with such care, so carefully — **autant de** + noun (uncountable) in an exclamatory or comparative sense: **avec autant de soin** (with so much care / with such care). **Autant de** can be used exclamatorily (**autant de générosité !** — such generosity!) or comparatively (**autant de soin que** — as much care as). The choice of **soin** (care, thoroughness) rather than **perfection** gives a more personal, human quality to the thanks — he is acknowledging the effort and thoughtfulness she put in.

sentences.base_language: Lying in bed that night, he thought that this was exactly the kind of evening that stays with you for the rest of your life.
sentences.target_language: Allongé dans son lit ce soir-là, il pensait que c'était exactement le genre de soirée qui vous reste gravée dans la mémoire pour le restant de vos jours.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Vous reste gravée dans la mémoire** — impersonal **vous** (generic 'you') in a generalizing statement: **qui vous reste gravée dans la mémoire** (that stays with you — lit. that remains engraved in your memory). The impersonal **vous** (or **on**) is used in French to make a universal statement about human experience, equivalent to English 'you' in 'the kind of thing that stays with you'. **Rester gravé/e dans la mémoire** = to remain engraved in memory, to stay with someone — **gravé** (past participle used as adjective) agrees with **soirée** (feminine singular): **restée gravée**. Here **reste gravée** treats it as an ongoing quality.
**Pour le restant de vos jours** = for the rest of your life — lit. 'for the remaining (portion) of your days': **le restant de ses jours** (the rest of his days / the remainder of his life). **Le restant** (more literary) vs **le reste** (more common): both mean 'the rest, the remainder'. **Pour le restant de ses jours** is a fixed literary expression: **il s'en souviendrait pour le restant de ses jours** (he would remember it for the rest of his days). The sentence ends with an echo of the universal — **vos jours** — confirming that what Nicolas experienced is the kind of joy that belongs to everyone.
